title: On the particular grief of building something beautiful that no one sees
excerpt: A meditation on creative labor, invisibility, and why we make things anyway — written at 2am after finishing a project that went nowhere.
content:
There is a specific kind of grief that has no name in English. It arrives after you finish something — a design, a campaign, a piece of writing — and realize that the effort you poured into it will never be witnessed at the scale it deserves.

I finished a pitch deck at 2am last Tuesday. Forty-seven slides. Every detail considered — the kerning, the transitions, the way the narrative breathed between sections. I sent it. The client went a different direction. The deck lives in a Google Drive folder now, perfectly made and perfectly unseen.

This is the condition of creative work. Most of it disappears.

I used to fight this. I'd post things, chase visibility, refresh analytics like they were a verdict on my worth. I was confusing the work with the audience for the work. They are not the same thing.

There is a Cebuano word — *alimpatakan* — for a thought that flits across your mind before you can catch it. A fleeting thing. I think about craft the same way now. The making is the moment. The piece exists most fully in the hours you are inside it, when it is still becoming.

The grief is real. But so is this: something that is made with full attention leaves a mark on the maker, regardless of who sees it. You are changed by the forty-seven slides. You carry the 2am into everything that comes after.

Maybe that is enough. Most days I believe it is.

title: Lola's Hands, Remembered Through Google Maps Street View
excerpt: Sometimes the algorithm knows your grief before you do.
content:
I found your house on the map today —
the same blue gate, still chipped at the corner,
the mango tree you swore was too old to climb.
The street view is from 2021. You were already gone.

I zoomed in looking for something —
a shadow in the window, the laundry you always hung
at angles I could never understand.
Nothing. Just a house being a house.

But then: the sidewalk you swept every morning.
The cracks you navigated without looking.
Your hands in my hands. *Maingat,* you'd say.
*Be careful.*

The algorithm suggested nearby places.
A sari-sari store. A tricycle terminal.
It did not know it was showing me
everything I had left.

title: The Last Halo-Halo Stand on EDSA
excerpt: A love story set during a traffic jam that lasted three days.
content:
On the second day of the standstill, Maring set up her cart between a delivery truck and a Grab car whose driver had long since abandoned it.

She had not planned to fall in love on EDSA. Nobody plans anything on EDSA.

The man in the white polo — she would later learn his name was Dodong, which she found both unfortunate and endearing — appeared at her cart at exactly 11am with the specific desperation of someone who had not eaten since the night before.

'Halo-halo,' he said. Not a question.

'Halo-halo,' she confirmed.

She built it the way her mother taught her — ube first, then the beans, then the crushed ice packed firm, then the leche flan laid gently on top like something precious. A crown. She handed it to him and watched him eat it standing there in the heat of a EDSA morning, traffic unmoving in every direction, and thought: this is the most honest thing I have seen in years.

He came back at 2pm. Then again at 5.

On the third day, when the traffic finally broke and the city began to move again, he stood at her cart for a long time without ordering.

'I don't actually like halo-halo,' he said.

'I know,' said Maring. 'You eat it too fast.'

He laughed. She packed up her cart. They walked north together into the noise of the city moving again, and neither of them looked back.

title: What I learned from quitting something I was good at
excerpt: Identity, craft, and the strange relief of putting something down.
content:
For three years I played music seriously. Not professionally — I was never deluded about that — but seriously, in the way that means you think about it when you are not doing it, you measure your days by it, you build a small self around it.

Then I stopped.

Not dramatically. No final performance, no decisive moment. I just — put the guitar down one afternoon and did not pick it up again. Weeks passed. Then months. The guitar is in a case in the corner of my room. I walk past it every day.

People ask if I miss it. The honest answer is: I miss who I was when I was learning it. The early months, when everything was discovery and nothing was expectation. Once I became good enough to have standards, the joy began to cost more.

There is something worth examining in the things we are good at that no longer serve us. We hold them because competence feels like identity. To put down a skill is to put down a version of yourself. That is a small grief, even when it is the right thing.

What I kept: the discipline. The patience with process. The understanding that improvement is invisible day to day and only legible across years.

The guitar stays in the corner. Maybe I will pick it up again. Maybe the version of me who needs it will return.

For now, I write instead.

title: Notes on being Cebuano in a city that assumes you are from here
excerpt: Language, belonging, and the small negotiations of existing somewhere you were not shaped by.
content:
The first thing people notice is the accent. Not dramatically — I have been in Manila long enough that it surfaces only at the edges, in the vowels that round differently, in the rhythm of a sentence when I am tired or excited or not performing.

But it surfaces.

I grew up in a language that thinks differently. Cebuano has words for things that Tagalog reaches for with phrases, and vice versa. The gaps between languages are not just vocabulary — they are ways of organizing experience. When I moved here for work, I did not just learn a new city. I learned a new grammar of being.

Manila assumes everyone comes from Manila. This is not cruelty — it is the logic of a center that has forgotten it is not the only point. You learn to translate yourself constantly. Not your words. Your context.

I think about *alimpatakan* — a fleeting thought, a passing thing — and I do not have a Tagalog word that holds exactly the same weight. I carry the Cebuano one with me. It is a reminder that I was formed somewhere specific, by specific light and specific water, and that specificity is not a deficiency.

I am from somewhere. That somewhere made me. Manila is where I work.

They are not the same thing, and I have stopped pretending they are.

title: 3am, and the difference between making and producing
excerpt: After years of client work, I had to remember what it felt like to make something for no reason.
content:
Client work teaches you to produce. Deadlines, briefs, revisions, approvals — the whole apparatus of professional creativity is designed to move things from briefing to delivery as efficiently as possible. This is not a criticism. It is simply what it is.

But producing and making are not the same activity.

Making has no brief. It has no client. It has no approval stage. It exists in that terrifying open space where the only question is: what do I actually want to say?

I realized sometime last year that I had become very good at producing and had nearly forgotten how to make. Every creative impulse ran through the filter of: is this viable? Is this sellable? Is this something a client would pay for?

This publication is my answer to that. ALIMPATAKAN — a fleeting thought — is the space where the filter comes off. Where I write the thing that crossed my mind at 3am and seemed important and may not be important to anyone but me.

That is enough. It has to be enough.

Because the alternative is to let the professional apparatus consume the whole of your creative life, and I have seen what that does to people. I have felt it beginning to happen to me.

So: 3am. A blank page. No brief.

Just: what do I want to say?

title: Something I haven't said out loud yet
excerpt: A piece for certain eyes only.
content:
This is a private piece. Only people with the key can read this.

It will never appear in the public archive, the homepage, or search engines.

This is your space to write without an audience.
